name: Aluminum
summary: Day 51. LME $3,557/mt (-2.40% April 17, off the $3,613.75 April 16 high). Hormuz-open announcement triggered a modest reversal but aluminum remains materially above pre-crisis — market still pricing persistent Gulf damage. Price fell only 1.6% from April 16 peak despite Brent -7.6% single session, reinforcing the structural thesis: aluminum repair/restart is 12-18 months. Gulf production damage unresolved: Borouge (UAE) shut since April 5; ALBA (Bahrain) 19% offline; EGA (UAE) Al Taweelah damage unrepaired. ~9% of global aluminum capacity at risk. Divergence vs energy (aluminum -1.6% from peak vs XLE -2.8% 1D alone) remains the tell — physical-damage pricing survives deal optimism. Hindalco, Vedanta benefit from sustained LME premium; Tata Motors, Maruti, Bajaj Auto face continued input cost pressure.

current.snapshot_note: April 17 close. Hormuz-open repricing triggered modest reversal but structural Gulf-damage premium intact. Compare to Brent -7.6% 1D — aluminum's resilience confirms physical-supply-damage pricing.

signals.content: IRGC claimed attacks on ALBA (Bahrain) and EGA (UAE) aluminum facilities in retaliation for US-Israeli strikes on Iranian steel plants. ALBA confirmed the attack — two employees injured. EGA's Al Taweelah site sustained significant damage, six people injured. ALBA had already initiated 'controlled and safe shutdown' of ~19% capacity (Lines 1-3) due to Hormuz-related disruptions. EGA's Al Taweelah produced 1.6 million tonnes of cast metal in 2025. Gulf aluminum output represents ~9% of global production. (Sources: The National Abu Dhabi, Al Jazeera, AlCircle, Business Standard, Gulf Business)

LME aluminum surged to $3,492-$3,500/mt — four-year high — following the ALBA/EGA attacks. Iranian attacks on UAE and Bahrain aluminum plants have heightened supply risks. The National reported aluminum prices 'likely to remain high' as long as Gulf disruption risks persist. This is a new category in Iran's targeting expansion: military → energy → industrial (aluminum). Aluminum is critical input for auto manufacturing, construction, aerospace, and packaging. India imports significant aluminum — cost pressure on Indian auto and construction sectors. (Sources: The National, LME data, Financial Content/MarketMinute)

Aluminum LME at $3,422/mt (-0.9% 1D, +4.3% monthly, +13.7% 3M). Borouge — the world's largest single-site polyolefin plant — shut down in UAE from interception debris on April 5. ALBA aluminum plant in Bahrain was struck earlier in the war and 19% capacity remains shut. Gulf aluminum/petrochemical production now facing DUAL threat: direct IRGC strike risk AND collateral damage from missile interception debris. This expands the risk profile — even successful air defense creates industrial casualties. ~9% of global aluminum production at risk across Gulf facilities. (Sources: The National, LME, Al Jazeera)

Gulf aluminum exposure unchanged: ALBA (Bahrain) and EGA (UAE) remain in the cumulative damage envelope. April 6 UAE intercepted 9 BMs / 50 drones; Saudi Arabia intercepted 7 BMs over Eastern Province with debris near energy facilities. None of the smelters have confirmed direct hits but the persistent Gulf-energy targeting pattern increases tail risk. Aluminum LME $3,422/mt +6.4% 1M / +14.3% 3M holding the war premium.

Day 50. LME ALUMINUM $3,557/mt (-2.40% 1D from $3,613.75) — first material pullback on Hormuz-open repricing [CONFIRMED price action]. Iran FM Araghchi's late-Friday declaration that Hormuz is 'completely open' triggered broad crisis-unwind (Brent -7.6%, WTI -11.3%, heating oil -12.8%) but aluminum fell only 2.40% — confirming the differentiation thesis. The structural signal remains intact: $3,557 is still +17% from the 3M start and only -1.6% off the April 16 peak, while Brent is -24% from its March 27 $112.57 peak. The commodity market is PRICING Gulf aluminum damage as persistent regardless of ceasefire outcome. Borouge, ALBA, EGA all without public restart announcements. For Indian autos (Tata, Maruti, Bajaj), input cost pressure only partially relieved by the shift. If Monday's Islamabad talks deliver and Gulf smelter restart announcements follow, expect a larger move to $3,400-3,450. If no smelter announcements by April 22, the wedge validates sustained +15% war premium. (Sources: LME, Trading Economics, Reuters commodities)

Day 49. LME ALUMINUM MADE NEW HIGH AT $3,613.75/mt (+1.1% 1D, +9.9% 1M, +19.1% 3M) — RALLYING INTO DEAL RHETORIC, NOT WITH IT [CONFIRMED price action]. The 9-day price evolution since last signal ($3,422 April 7 → $3,613.75 April 16, +5.6%) is materially inconsistent with the broader de-escalation narrative driving S&P to 7,022 ATH and Brent futures flat at $95. This is the signal: the aluminum market is pricing that Gulf supply damage is PERSISTENT regardless of whether the Iran ceasefire holds. The physical basis: Borouge (UAE, world's largest single-site polyolefin plant, ~$60B) still shut from April 5 interception debris — no public restart announcement; ALBA (Bahrain) 19% capacity still offline; EGA (UAE) Al Taweelah damage from March 29 IRGC strike unrepaired. The structural thesis is that even a clean Trump-Iran ceasefire extension does not restore ~9% of global primary aluminum capacity on a near-term timeline — the repair/restart cycle for damaged smelters is 12-18 months minimum. Co-movement pattern confirms the differentiation: aluminum +9.9% 1M while energy (XLE) is -3.1% 1M and Brent futures are -8.1% 1M. The commodity that should theoretically benefit MOST from Iran demand destruction (lower energy input cost) is instead leading the physical-damage pricing. For Indian markets: Hindalco and Vedanta (primary producers) structurally benefit from sustained LME premium + domestic supply advantage; Tata Motors, Maruti, Bajaj Auto face input cost pressure that compounds their existing Brent-linked diesel/logistics costs. (Sources: LME, Bloomberg, Reuters commodities)

edges.context: IRGC struck ALBA and EGA in retaliation for US-Israeli strikes on Iranian steel plants. New industrial targeting category. Interception debris adds collateral damage risk even without direct hits.
ALBA (Aluminium Bahrain) — 1.62M mt/year. 19% capacity still shut. Two workers injured in IRGC attack.
EGA (Emirates Global Aluminium) — Al Taweelah produced 1.6M mt in 2025. Significant damage from IRGC attack. Borouge shut from interception debris April 5. Dual risk profile.
India imports aluminum. LME at $3,422/mt increases input costs for Indian auto (Tata Motors, Maruti), construction, and packaging sectors.

trigger_points.condition: Gulf aluminum production drops >25% sustained (ALBA + EGA combined)
trigger_points.mechanism: Would significantly tighten global aluminum supply. LME could hit $4,000+. Indian auto and construction sectors face margin compression. Hindalco (India's largest aluminum company) could benefit as domestic producer but faces higher energy costs.
